name: Garrovillas de Alconétar from Navas del Madroño – circular by Cáseres and Arroyo de la Luz
name_original: Garrovillas de Alconétar desde Navas del Madroño – circular por Cáseres y Arroyo de la Luz
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Navas Del Madroño traverse an undulating landscape characterized by granite rocks and soft hills, with an average altitude of 428 meters. The terrain features varied inclines and descents, suitable for road cycling. The region is dominated by Mediterranean vegetation, including extensive dehesas with holm oaks and cork oaks, as well as olive groves. The Rivera de Araya, a significant geological fault, also contributes to the area's natural features. Best road cycling routes around Navas Del Madroño The most popular road cycling route is Garrovillas de Alconétar from Navas del Madroño – circular by Cáseres and Arroyo de la Luz, a 59.6 miles (95.9 km) trail that takes 4 hours 15 minutes to complete. This route offers a circular journey through the varied landscapes connecting several towns. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Garrovillas de Alconétar from Alcántara – circular via Brozas and Navas del Madroño, a moderate 51.0 miles (82.1 km) path. This route connects Alcántara, Brozas, and Navas del Madroño, providing diverse scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Salorino and Membrío from Brozas – circular by the río Salor, a 50.2 miles (80.8 km) trail leading through the River Salor area, often completed in about 3 hours 53 minutes. Road cycling around Navas Del Madroño is defined by gently undulating hills, dehesas, and the Rivera de Araya. The network offers options for moderate and difficult ability levels. The routes in Navas Del Madroño are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Navas Del Madroño's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Navas Del Madroño?
A: There are 11 road cycling routes available around Navas Del Madroño. These routes cater to various skill levels, with 5 moderate and 6 difficult options.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling near Navas Del Madroño?
A: The region around Navas Del Madroño features a gently undulating landscape with granite rocks and soft hills, averaging 428 meters in altitude. You'll experience varied inclines and descents, predominantly through Mediterranean vegetation, extensive &lt;em&gt;dehesas&lt;/em&gt; with holm oaks and cork oaks, and olive groves. The &lt;a href="https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Navas_del_Madro%C3%B1o" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rivera de Araya&lt;/a&gt;, a significant geological fault, also contributes to the area's natural features.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Navas Del Madroño?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are circular. For example, the route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1057943768/garrovillas-de-alconetar-from-navas-del-madrono-circular-by-caseres-and-arroyo-de-la-luz" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Garrovillas de Alconétar from Navas del Madroño – circular by Cáseres and Arroyo de la Luz&lt;/a&gt; offers a comprehensive loop through the varied landscapes, connecting several towns. Another option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058751660/garrovillas-de-alconetar-from-alcantara-circular-via-brozas-and-navas-del-madrono" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Garrovillas de Alconétar from Alcántara – circular via Brozas and Navas del Madroño&lt;/a&gt;, which provides diverse scenery connecting Alcántara, Brozas, and Navas del Madroño.

Q: What natural features or landscapes will I encounter on road cycling routes?
A: Road cycling routes will take you through picturesque &lt;em&gt;dehesas&lt;/em&gt; (wooded pastures) dominated by holm oaks and cork oaks, as well as olive groves. You might also pass by the &lt;a href="https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Navas_del_Madro%C3%B1o" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rivera de Araya&lt;/a&gt;, a notable geological feature, and experience the unique stone pine forest between Navas del Madroño and Garrovillas. The Sierra de Santo Domingo also defines the municipality's geographical setting.

Q: Are there any interesting landmarks or attractions to see along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, several points of interest can be found near the routes. You might spot the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4760879" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Osborne Bull on the N-630&lt;/a&gt;. The town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5325565" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Brozas&lt;/a&gt;, with its &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5582877" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Main Square&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5676660" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Convent of San Antonio de Padua&lt;/a&gt;, is also accessible. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5912881" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of Altagracia&lt;/a&gt; is another notable religious monument in the vicinity.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Navas Del Madroño?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 3 reviews. Cyclists often praise the gently undulating hills, the serene &lt;em&gt;dehesas&lt;/em&gt;, and the diverse landscapes that define road cycling around Navas Del Madroño.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Navas Del Madroño?
A: The Extremadura region, including Navas Del Madroño, benefits from a pleasant Mediterranean climate, making it suitable for year-round cycling. However, spring and autumn generally offer the most comfortable temperatures for longer rides, avoiding the peak heat of summer.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes for beginners in Navas Del Madroño?
A: The road cycling routes around Navas Del Madroño are primarily categorized as moderate or difficult. There are no routes specifically classified as 'easy' in this guide, suggesting that a basic level of fitness and experience with varied inclines is beneficial.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: While specific cafes are not listed for each route, many routes connect or pass through towns and villages like Navas Del Madroño, Brozas, and Garrovillas de Alconétar. These settlements typically offer opportunities for refreshments, allowing you to refuel during your ride.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the River Salor area?
A: Yes, the route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058780024/salorino-and-membrio-from-brozas-circular-by-the-rio-salor" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salorino and Membrío from Brozas – circular by the río Salor&lt;/a&gt; is a 50.2-mile (80.8 km) trail that leads directly through the River Salor area, offering scenic views of this hydrological feature.

Q: What is the longest road cycling route available?
A: The longest road cycling route featured is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1057943768/garrovillas-de-alconetar-from-navas-del-madrono-circular-by-caseres-and-arroyo-de-la-luz" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Garrovillas de Alconétar from Navas del Madroño – circular by Cáseres and Arroyo de la Luz&lt;/a&gt;, spanning 59.6 miles (95.9 km) and typically taking around 4 hours and 15 minutes to complete.

Q: What is the highest point reached on the road cycling routes?
A: While specific route elevations vary, the region itself has an average altitude of 428 meters, with the highest natural point being Cabeza de Araya at 518 meters. Routes will feature varied inclines and descents within this range, offering a good workout.
tips: Garrovillas de Alconétar is a small town in the Tajo-Salor region. A strategic place on the left bank of the Tagus River, near its confluence with the Almonte River. Stop at the Plaza Mayor for food and drinks and admire the heritage buildings that surround the Plaza.

A brand converted into national identity. The Osborne bulls were billboards for a brandy brand. In recent years the brand abandoned the use of these billboards to promote itself and the bulls remained as a characteristic element of Spanish roads recognized nationally and internationally.

The peculiar structure of this bullring testifies to its historical past, which has earned it being declared a Site of Cultural Interest.
The Cáceres Bullring, also known as “Coso de la Era de los Mártires”, is one of the oldest in Spain.
Its construction began in 1844 and the works were completed two years later. The first bullfight was held on August 6, 1846, with bulls led by D. Gaspar Muñoz.
The square is built with granite and masonry walls, it has a capacity of 8,000 spectators spread over three floors.
In addition to bullfighting celebrations, it is also used for other types of events such as music concerts.

The San Francisco Bridge in Cáceres is a historic structure that has undergone several transformations over the years. Originally built in the 16th century, this medieval bridge is located in the eastern part of the city, on the old road to Trujillo. The bridge was built to level the terrain of a promenade next to the San Francisco convent and was adorned with two rows of black poplars planted in 1752.

Malpartida de Cáceres is a small town along the national highway N-521 west of Cáceres. The town is the gateway to Los Barruecos Natural Monument, a series of lakes surrounded by a landscape of granite rocks.

The Church of the Assumption is considered one of the finest examples of late Gothic architecture. For this reason, both the church and its main altarpiece were declared cultural heritage sites in 1981.

Navas del Madroño is a small rural town located at the crossroads of three cattle ravines that lead to the capital Cáceres between the Alcántara and Alconétar bridges. Today, when you cycle to the city, don't forget to visit its most characteristic monuments, the Church of Nuestra Señora de la O, and stop for a coffee in the Plaza de Constitución.
